topic: A person is asked to give a speech at their parent's retirement party
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation had arrived like a declaration of war. A speech. At my parents' retirement bash. I stared at the embossed card, the paper feeling coarse and insulting beneath my thumb. They had somehow managed to select me, the reluctant spokesperson for their celebrated career, when I was currently wrestling with a particularly stubborn jar lid. A slow, hot pressure began to build behind my sternum, tightening with every passing minute. I paced the living room, the hardwood floor echoing the frantic rhythm of my heartbeat. When my mother called me in for a final pep talk, her smile was too bright, too saccharine. "You'll be wonderful," she chirped, completely oblivious to the tremor in my hands. I just nodded, the silence in my throat a physical barrier. I felt a distinct urge to slam my fist onto the nearest surface, to shatter the delicate glassware.

[story 2]

"Just tell them you’re happy," my father had urged, his voice laced with a forced cheerfulness as he adjusted his tie for the tenth time. I watched him, the familiar sight of his practiced composure grating on my nerves. Every word he spoke about the next few years of leisure sounded like a deliberate mockery. I gripped my glass of lukewarm punch so tightly my knuckles turned white. The air in the hallway seemed too thick, too heavy to breathe. Inside, a furious churning took hold; a burning sensation radiating from my chest outward. I could feel the blood pounding in my ears, a frantic drumbeat against the suffocating quiet of my own mind. I wanted to scream that I didn't want this, that I wanted nothing to do with their celebration, but the words caught, trapped behind a sudden, blinding surge of heat.

[story 3]

The stage felt like a precipice. I stood backstage, the spotlight already humming, and a wave of sheer, unadulterated frustration washed over me, leaving me momentarily unsteady. The applause from the crowd sounded distant, muffled, like noise from another planet. I looked at the script—a pre-approved, sanitized tribute—and felt a violent rejection of the very words. My jaw was clenched so hard I tasted copper. When the host called my name, I walked onto the stage with a stiff, unnatural gait. My voice, when it finally emerged, was unnervingly level, a thin, brittle sound that carried an unmistakable edge of pure, contained fury. I could feel the collective scrutiny of the room, and for a fleeting second, I wanted to turn around and walk straight out the nearest exit.
